pages.title: Welcome to Incident Response & Reporting for Agencies
pages.subtitle: Learn how Ohio public employees should recognize, report, and support response to cybersecurity incidents.

video.caption: Introduction to incident response and reporting for Ohio agencies (replace later)

pages.title: Why this matters in Ohio
pages.text:
Cyber incidents in government do more than disrupt computers. They can interrupt public services, delay operations, affect public safety, expose sensitive information, and reduce public trust.

Ohio law requires political subdivisions to take cybersecurity seriously. That includes adopting a cybersecurity program and reporting incidents through the proper channels.

Fast reporting helps the right people respond sooner, preserve evidence, coordinate assistance, and reduce damage.

pages.title: Ohio cybersecurity program requirements
pages.text:
Ohio Revised Code 9.64 requires political subdivisions to adopt a cybersecurity program appropriate for their size and risk.

A cybersecurity program should address:

  • Critical functions and risks
  • Threat detection and monitoring
  • Incident response procedures
  • Recovery and ongoing security measures
  • Training for employees based on job duties

Each organization can design its own program, but these areas must be covered.

pages.title: Implementation deadlines in Ohio
pages.text:
Ohio's cybersecurity program requirement has implementation deadlines for political subdivisions.

  • Counties and cities: by January 1, 2026
  • All other political subdivisions: by July 1, 2026

Regular Auditor of State audits may review whether these requirements are being met.

pages.title: What counts as a cyber incident
pages.text:
Not every technical issue is a cyber incident, but employees should report suspicious activity early instead of deciding on their own that it is harmless.

Examples that may require escalation include:

  • Ransomware or locked files
  • Unauthorized access or suspicious logins
  • Business email compromise or account compromise
  • Data exposure or suspicious transfers
  • Malware alerts or strange system behavior
  • System outages caused by suspected malicious activity

When in doubt, report it through the agency’s process.

pages.title: How to prepare before an incident happens
pages.text:
Incident response works better when key decisions are made before the emergency.

Preparation stepWhy it matters
Know your reporting chainStaff should know exactly who to call first
Keep contact lists currentDelays happen when numbers and emails are outdated
Identify critical systemsHelps responders focus on the most important services first
Plan for backups and recoveryRecovery is faster when backup and restoration roles are clear
Practice the processSimple walkthroughs help people respond calmly under pressure

Preparation reduces confusion, shortens response time, and improves recovery.

pages.title: Build a simple incident response file
pages.text:
Every agency should keep a simple incident response file or quick-reference sheet that staff can find quickly.

A basic file should include:

  • Internal contacts such as IT, leadership, legal, communications, and department leads
  • External contacts such as OCIC, the Auditor of State, FBI, and IC3 when applicable
  • Critical systems and services that matter most
  • Basic first-action steps for suspected ransomware, account compromise, malware, or data exposure
  • Where to store evidence such as screenshots, emails, timestamps, and device details
  • Escalation rules explaining who can approve outages, network isolation, public statements, or outside support

Keep it simple. A short, usable file is better than a long plan nobody can find during an emergency.

video.caption: Introduction to incident response and reporting for Ohio agencies (replace later)

pages.title: First actions during a suspected incident
pages.text:
Employees should know the difference between helping and making the situation worse.

  1. Pause and stop interacting with suspicious files, systems, or messages
  2. Report the issue immediately through the approved internal chain
  3. Do not delete evidence, wipe devices, or quietly try to fix the problem yourself
  4. If instructed by your agency, disconnect affected devices from the network
  5. Follow guidance from IT, security, leadership, legal counsel, and incident leads

Quick escalation and evidence preservation are critical.

pages.title: Ohio reporting deadlines under state law
pages.text:
If a political subdivision discovers a cybersecurity incident, Ohio law requires reporting to state authorities on a timeline.

  • Report to the Ohio Cyber Integration Center (OCIC) within 7 days
  • Report to the Auditor of State within 30 days

These reports support both operational response and legal compliance. Your agency should know who is responsible for making them, but every employee should understand that incidents must be escalated quickly rather than ignored or delayed.

Helpful contacts:

AgencyDeadlineContact information
Ohio Cyber Integration Center (OCIC)Within 7 daysOCIC@dps.ohio.gov
614-387-1089
OCIC reporting page
Ohio Auditor of StateWithin 30 daysCyber@ohioauditor.gov
Auditor of State cybersecurity reporting page
Cybersecurity reporting form

References: Ohio Revised Code 9.64 | Ohio Auditor of State cybersecurity policy guidance

pages.title: Who to contact and in what order
pages.text:
Most employees should follow internal reporting first, then let authorized staff handle state and federal reporting.

ContactWhy they matter
Internal IT / security / leadershipUsually your first stop for escalation and coordination
OCICOhio coordination and incident intake
Auditor of StateRequired state reporting process
FBI / IC3Cybercrime reporting and investigative support

Internal procedures still come first unless your plan says otherwise.

pages.title: Ransomware: report, isolate, do not panic
pages.text:
Ransomware can spread quickly and interrupt public services.

If a device appears infected, the priority is to report the incident immediately and follow agency instructions. Ohio guidance also recommends removing an infected computer from the network or internet and isolating other affected devices when appropriate.

Do not assume paying a ransom is the answer. Ohio’s law requires public action before a political subdivision pays a ransomware demand, and that decision must be justified as being in the public interest.

Report quickly, preserve evidence, and follow coordinated instructions.

pages.title: Public records and confidentiality
pages.text:
Employees should not assume incident details can be shared freely.

For Ohio political subdivisions, records, documents, and reports related to a cybersecurity program, cybersecurity framework, or reported cybersecurity incident under ORC 9.64 are not public records.

That means employees should avoid casually sharing incident details, screenshots, or internal response information unless authorized to do so. Sensitive information should be handled through approved channels only.

Reference: Ohio Revised Code 9.64

image.alt: Confidential cyber incident information graphic (replace later)

pages.title: How Reporting Supports Response and Investigation
pages.text:
Reporting is not just about checking a box. It helps your organization and can also support broader investigations and defense.

  • Coordinate response assistance
  • Preserve investigative leads
  • Connect your incident to larger attack patterns
  • Warn other organizations facing similar threats
  • Support recovery and accountability

Hiding or delaying an incident can make the damage worse.

pages.title: Key Ohio and Federal contacts
pages.text:
Your agency should maintain current contact information in its response procedures so the right people can be reached quickly during an incident.

Common resources may include:

  • OCIC for state incident reporting and coordination
  • Auditor of State for required Ohio reporting processes
  • IC3 for internet crime reporting
  • FBI field office for investigative support when directed by your agency plan
  • Third-party incident response or forensic support if your organization relies on an outside cybersecurity company, managed service provider, cyber insurance partner, or legal/vendor response team

Some organizations may also need to contact an external cybersecurity company to help with containment, forensic review, recovery, or evidence preservation. If that applies, those contacts should already be listed in the response file.

In a real incident, the best contact list is the one your organization already prepared before the emergency.

Employees should still follow internal agency procedures first unless directed otherwise.

References: Ohio Auditor of State Cybersecurity Resources | IC3

pages.title: What employees should remember
pages.text:
You do not need to be the incident commander to make a difference.

  • Report early
  • Do not hide problems
  • Do not destroy evidence
  • Use approved channels
  • Support a coordinated response

Strong incident response starts with people recognizing warning signs and taking the reporting process seriously.
